abstract: The traditional use of planar (hetero)aromatic rings in drug discovery often entails suboptimal physicochemical properties and metabolic vulnerability. Consequently, $sp^3$-hybridized caged hydrocarbons—most notably [1.1.1]-bicyclopentanes (BCPs), [2.1.1]-bicyclohexanes (BCHs), [3.1.1]-bicycloheptanes (BCHeps), and cubanes—have emerged as essential bioisosteres capable of mimicking various arene substitution patterns while improving solubility and metabolic stability. This review highlights recent breakthroughs in the light-driven synthesis and functionalization of these high-value 3D scaffolds. We examine a wide array of photochemical strategies, ranging from visible-light photocatalysis and energy transfer (EnT) manifolds to direct excitation and electron donor-acceptor (EDA) complex activation. Particular focus is given to the radical-mediated opening of propellanes, the precise installation of functional groups at bridgehead and bridge positions, and the development of stereoselective and DNA-compatible methodologies. By providing a comprehensive overview of the current synthetic landscape and mechanistic underpinnings, we also discuss remaining challenges and future opportunities for these rigid, saturated frameworks in medicinal chemistry and materials science.
